title: QUIC CRT client-role 1-RTT readiness architecture
purpose: Describe how the client-role Finished path publishes the first application packet-protection material only after peer Finished verification, so the supported active-loopback data-path can open honestly without widening the supported surface.
risks: If OneRtt keys are published before PeerFinishedVerified, the supported data-path would appear usable before the Finished proof boundary.
If the bridge gate remains open to premature direct updates, future callers could fabricate client 1-RTT readiness without the peer proof.
If the benchmark and requirement-home tests do not exercise the client path after certificate-policy acceptance, regressions in the active-loopback seam can be missed.
design_summary: The client Finished path now has a narrow, proof-gated 1-RTT readiness seam. After the managed key schedule verifies the peer Finished, it surfaces the local Finished bytes, publishes `KeysAvailable` for `OneRtt`, and publishes directional 1-RTT packet-protection material in that order. The bridge state rejects premature `OneRtt` key publication unless `PeerFinishedVerified` is already set, which keeps the active application-data seam honest while leaving the supported public boundary unchanged. The runtime still requires the existing Active-phase transition before application streams or application packets can flow.
